projects.title: Unit 9 Project — Coordinate Plane & Integers in Action
projects.desc: Apply graphing, integers, and the coordinate plane to a real-world capstone challenge for Unit 9.

projects.title: Number Line Jumper
projects.desc: Compare and order integers by jumping along the number line (6.NS.7).

projects.title: Sub-Zero Ledger & Altitude Compass
projects.desc: Order positive and negative values using altitude and below-zero readings (6.NS.7).

title: Compare and Order Integers
contentObjective: I can compare and order integers using a number line.
languageObjective: I can explain my reasoning using the words compare, order, greater than, and less than.

turnAndTalk.question: Why is -3 less than 2, even though 3 looks like a bigger number than 2?
turnAndTalk.kernel: -3 is less than 2 because on the number line ___.

stems.en: On the number line, -3 is to the ___ of 2.
stems.es: En la recta numerica, -3 esta a la ___ de 2.

stems.en: Numbers get smaller as you move ___.
stems.es: Los numeros se hacen mas pequenos a medida que te mueves ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Student explains that -3 is farther left on the number line than 2, and numbers decrease moving left.
turnAndTalk.extend: Push students to explain why every negative number is less than every positive number.
extendStems: Every negative number is less than every positive number because ___.
On the number line, all negatives are ___ of all positives.

turnAndTalk.question: Order these temperatures from coldest to warmest: -8, 3, -1, 5. How does the number line help you put them in order?
turnAndTalk.kernel: I can order the integers by looking at their position on ___.

stems.en: The coldest temperature is ___ because it is farthest ___.
stems.es: La temperatura mas fria es ___ porque esta mas a la ___.

stems.en: From least to greatest, the order is ___.
stems.es: De menor a mayor, el orden es ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Student orders -8, -1, 3, 5 and uses left-to-right position on the number line to justify the order.
turnAndTalk.extend: Ask students to write a comparison statement using < or > for two of the temperatures and read it aloud.
extendStems: Reading left to right on the number line, ___ comes before ___.

turnAndTalk.question: How does knowing a number's position on the number line tell you whether to use the < or > symbol?
turnAndTalk.kernel: The number farther to the left is always ___.

stems.en: The number on the left is ___ than the number on the right.
stems.es: El numero de la izquierda es ___ que el numero de la derecha.

stems.en: The open side of the symbol points to ___.
stems.es: El lado abierto del simbolo apunta a ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Student connects left position to 'less than' and right position to 'greater than', and uses the symbol correctly.
turnAndTalk.extend: Push students to generalize a rule for comparing two negative numbers, like -7 and -2.
extendStems: When comparing two negatives, the one closer to zero is ___.

turnAndTalk.question: Two students disagree: one says -10 is greater than -2 because 10 is bigger. Who is right, and how do you know?

stems.en: I know because on the number line -10 is ___.
stems.es: Lo se porque en la recta numerica -10 esta ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Student explains -10 is less than -2 because it is farther left, correcting the 'bigger digits' misconception.
turnAndTalk.extend: Ask students to critique why the 'bigger digits means bigger number' idea fails for negatives.
extendStems: The 'bigger digits' idea fails for negatives because ___.
With negative numbers, more distance from zero actually means ___.

vocabulary.definition: To see if a number is bigger, smaller, or equal to another.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Ver si un número es mayor, menor o igual que otro.
vocabulary.visual: -3 < 2 because -3 is farther left on the number line than 2
vocabulary.cloze: To decide which number is larger or smaller is to ___ them.

vocabulary.definition: To put numbers in order, smallest to biggest or biggest to smallest.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Poner números en orden, de menor a mayor o de mayor a menor.
vocabulary.visual: Least to greatest: -5, -2, 0, 3, 7 (left to right on the number line)
vocabulary.cloze: To arrange numbers from least to greatest or greatest to least is to ___ them.

vocabulary.definition: Farther right on a number line. The > sign.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Más a la derecha en la recta numérica. El signo >.
vocabulary.visual: 4 > -1 because 4 is to the right of -1 on the number line
vocabulary.cloze: On a number line, a number farther to the right is ___ another number.

vocabulary.definition: Farther left on a number line. The < sign.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Más a la izquierda en la recta numérica. El signo <.
vocabulary.visual: -6 < -2 because -6 is farther left — it is farther from zero
vocabulary.cloze: On a number line, a number farther to the left is ___ another number.

vocabulary.definition: A whole number smaller than zero, like -5.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Un número entero menor que cero, como -5.
vocabulary.visual: -4 is 4 units to the LEFT of zero on the number line
vocabulary.cloze: An integer less than zero, like −6, is a ___.

examples.why: It is an integer less than zero.
It is greater than zero, so it is positive.

vocabulary.definition: Whole numbers and their opposites, like -2, -1, 0, 1, 2.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Números enteros y sus opuestos, como -2, -1, 0, 1, 2.
vocabulary.cloze: A whole number or its opposite is an ___.

launch.narrative: Captain Vega's crew found treasures at different depths underwater! The deeper the treasure, the more negative the elevation. A gold coin is at -2 meters, a silver crown is at -7 meters, and a ruby ring is at -5 meters. The crew needs to rank them from the shallowest (closest to the surface) to the deepest.
launch.contextImage: Underwater cross-section showing treasures at different depths below the ocean surface
noticePrompts: Which treasure is closest to the surface?
Is -7 greater than or less than -2?
How does the number line help you compare negative numbers?
wonderPrompts: Why does a bigger negative number mean a deeper location?
What if one treasure was above water — how would you compare it to the underwater ones?

conceptIntro.heading: How do you compare and order integers?
conceptIntro.intro: To compare integers means to see which one is greater or less. On a number line, numbers get bigger as you go right and smaller as you go left.
conceptIntro.keyIdea: The number farther to the left is always less; the number farther to the right is always greater.

iDo.title: Watch me
lines: I will compare -7 and -5. They are both negative, so I look at the number line.
-7 is 7 steps left of zero. -5 is only 5 steps left of zero.
-7 is farther to the left, so -7 is the smaller number.
I write -7 < -5. The open side of the symbol points to the bigger number, -5.

weDo.title: Let's try together
lines: Now let's compare -2 and 3. Which one is to the left on the number line? The -2 is left of zero.
Which one is to the right? The 3 is right of zero.
So which is greater, and what symbol do we use? 3 is greater, so -2 < 3.

youDo.title: Now it's your turn
lines: In the explore, plot the treasure depths like -2, -7, and -5, then order them from least to greatest.

explore.instructions: Plot the treasure depths on the number line. Then order them from least to greatest.
explore.label: Plot each treasure location and arrange them from least to greatest.

discourse.prompt: How does plotting on the number line help you compare and order integers?
discourse.sentenceFrame: On the number line, ___ is to the left of ___, so ___ < ___. The order from least to greatest is ___.

optional.stem: The crew marks four spots on the map: a tide pool at -3 m, a flag at 2 m, a cave at -7 m, and the boat deck at 0 m. Order them from least to greatest (deepest to highest).
optional.explanation: The deepest spot is the cave at -7 (farthest left), then the tide pool -3, then the boat deck 0, then the flag at 2. Least to greatest: -7, -3, 0, 2.

optional.stem: Three buried chests sit at -9 m, -4 m, and -15 m below sea level. Which chest is at the GREATEST elevation (closest to the surface)?
optional.explanation: Closer to zero means greater for negatives. -4 is only 4 below the surface, so -4 is the greatest of -9, -4, and -15.

optional.stem: On the map, a sunken lantern is at -8 m and a coral shelf is at -3 m. Which symbol makes the comparison true:  -8 ___ -3 ?
optional.explanation: -8 is farther left (deeper) than -3, so -8 is less than -3. The symbol is <.

optional.instructions: Sort each treasure marker into the right zone on the map: below sea level or above sea level.

optional.label: Match each treasure-map comparison to the symbol that makes it true.

optional.title: Level 2 Extension — The Mis-Ranked Treasure Map

workedExample.label: Problem
workedExample.work: Order the chest depths from least to greatest: -8 m and -15 m.

workedExample.label: Crew thinking
workedExample.work: 15 has bigger digits than 8, so -15 must be the bigger number. I'll list -8 first because it must be smaller.

optional.correctWork: For negative numbers, MORE distance from zero means SMALLER, not bigger. -15 is 15 below the surface (deeper and farther left), so -15 is actually less than -8. The correct order from least to greatest is -15, -8.
hints: Plot -8 and -15 on the number line. Which one is farther to the LEFT?
Bigger digits do NOT mean a bigger negative. The deeper chest (-15) is the least value.

approaching.explanation: -5 is farther to the left on the number line than -2, so -5 < -2 is true.
-8 is farthest to the left on the number line, making it the least (smallest) integer.
Absolute value is distance from 0, always positive. |−8| = 8.

approaching.label: Drag to order these integers from LEAST to GREATEST.
Plot -3, 1, -7, 4, and -1 on the number line to help you compare them.

onLevel.label: Plot these integers and write them in order from greatest to least: -8, 4, -3, 1, -1, 6

onLevel.instructions: Compare each pair of integers using < , > , or =.

onLevel.label: Sort these integers from GREATEST to LEAST.

onLevel.stem: Three cities have temperatures of -12°F, -5°F, and 3°F. Which city is warmest?
onLevel.explanation: 3°F is the greatest because it is farthest to the right on the number line. The order from coldest to warmest is -12, -5, 3.

extending.title: Find the Comparison Error

workedExample.label: Student thinking
workedExample.work: 8 is bigger than 3, so -8 must be greater than -3

extending.correctWork: With negative numbers, the number closer to zero is GREATER. -3 is closer to zero than -8, so -3 > -8. The student compared the digits without considering that negatives work in the opposite direction.
hints: Plot -3 and -8 on a number line. Which one is farther to the right?
For negatives, closer to zero means greater. -3 is only 3 units from zero, but -8 is 8 units away.

extending.prompt: A student says: 'Negative numbers are always less than positive numbers, so -100 is less than 1.' Is this correct? Now the student says: '-100 is less than -1 because 100 is greater than 1.' Is THIS correct? Explain the difference.
extending.sentenceFrame: The first statement is ___ because ___. The second statement is ___ because with negative numbers, ___. On the number line, -100 is ___ of -1, so ___.

extending.label: These integers represent bank account changes. Sort from the WORST change (biggest loss) to the BEST change (biggest gain).

optionalActivity.name: Integer Ranking Race
optionalActivity.intro: Race the treasure crew to rank depths and altitudes from least to greatest before the tide rises!

connect.scenario: A weather station recorded these temperatures during a winter week: Monday -4°F, Tuesday 2°F, Wednesday -7°F, Thursday -1°F, Friday 5°F. The station needs to report the coldest and warmest days.
connect.promptQuestion: How do you use integer comparison to find the coldest and warmest days?
connect.prompt: The coldest day was ___ at ___°F because ___. The warmest day was ___ at ___°F. I ordered them from coldest to warmest by ___.

exitTicket.stem: Which list shows the integers in order from LEAST to GREATEST?
exitTicket.explanation: -5 is the smallest (farthest left on the number line), then -2, then 0, then 3, then 7. The correct order from least to greatest is -5, -2, 0, 3, 7.
